name: scikit-optimize (Sequential Bayesian Optimization)

performance.notes: Sequential optimization; GP/RF/ET surrogate models; overhead small vs function evaluation

agent_readiness.notes: sklearn-compatible Bayesian optimization. from skopt import gp_minimize, forest_minimize. from skopt.space import Real, Integer, Categorical. space = [Real(1e-6, 1e-1, prior='log-uniform'), Integer(1, 50)]. result = gp_minimize(objective_fn, space, n_calls=100). result.x (best params), result.fun (best value). BayesSearchCV for sklearn pipelines: BayesSearchCV(estimator, param_grid, n_iter=50). Decorators: @use_named_args(space). Plots: plot_convergence, plot_objective. Active development status: sporadic (use Optuna for new projects).

gotchas: Development has been sporadic — use Optuna for actively maintained alternative
Sequential only — no parallel/async evaluation built-in
Limited to sklearn-compatible estimators for BayesSearchCV
Slower than Optuna's TPE for typical HPO workloads
best_when: sklearn pipeline hyperparameter optimization via BayesSearchCV
Teams already using scipy/sklearn and wanting Bayesian HPO
Legacy codebases that standardized on scikit-optimize
Educational use — simple, well-documented Bayesian optimization
avoid_when: New projects — Optuna is better maintained and more feature-rich
Parallel hyperparameter search (use Optuna with distributed backend)
summary: scikit-optimize provides sklearn-compatible Bayesian optimization. gp_minimize(objective, space, n_calls=100) — Gaussian Process surrogate. BayesSearchCV(estimator, param_grid) for sklearn pipelines. Space types: Real, Integer, Categorical. result.x for best parameters. Sporadic development — prefer Optuna for new projects. Best for teams in the sklearn ecosystem wanting Bayesian HPO with familiar APIs.
